message:
fix(cli): remove invalid JSON comment from diagnose remediation template

The remediation template contained `// or ["inference"]` which is invalid
JSON syntax. Moved the alternative option to a note after the code block
to prevent copy-paste parse errors.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"brenner_bot-nu8g","title":"EPIC: Artifact Generation - Research Briefs & Exports","description":"# Artifact Generation - Research Briefs & Exports

## Purpose
Transform ephemeral sessions into durable, shareable research artifacts that capture the intellectual work done.

## Why Artifacts Matter
The value of the Brenner Loop isn't just the thinking—it's the **record of the thinking**. Good artifacts:
1. Survive beyond the session
2. Can be shared with collaborators
3. Serve as institutional memory
4. Document the intellectual journey, not just conclusions

## Core Artifact Types

### 1. Research Brief
The primary output. A structured document containing:
- Executive Summary (2-3 sentences)
- Hypothesis Statement (current version)
- Hypothesis Evolution (how it changed and why)
- Discriminative Tests Identified (ranked by power)
- Agent Analysis (consensus and conflicts)
- Evidence Summary (if any tests completed)
- Brenner Principles Applied (which quotes, which operators)
- Recommended Next Steps

Format: Markdown with structured metadata header.

### 2. Hypothesis Card (Export)
Standalone export of a hypothesis with:
- Full card structure
- Version history
- Robustness score
- Confounds identified

Format: JSON (for import/programmatic use) or Markdown (for reading).

### 3. Evidence Ledger Export
Complete audit trail of:
- All tests recorded
- Confidence updates over time
- Sources and observations

Format: CSV (for analysis) or Markdown (for review).

### 4. Session Transcript
Full record of the session including:
- All operator applications
- All agent responses
- All user decisions
- Timeline of events

Format: Markdown with timestamps.

## Structured Output Schema

### Research Brief Metadata
```yaml
---
type: research_brief
version: 1.0
session_id: SM-2024-001
hypothesis_id: hyp-xyz
created_at: 2024-03-15T10:30:00Z
confidence: 37%
status: under_pressure
operators_applied:
  - level_split
  - exclusion_test
agents_consulted:
  - devils_advocate
  - experiment_designer
  - brenner_channeler
tests_identified: 5
tests_completed: 2
brenner_citations:
  - §42
  - §89
  - §73
---
```

### Export Formats
- **PDF**: For sharing with non-technical audiences
- **Markdown**: For git storage, collaboration
- **JSON**: For programmatic use, import/export
- **HTML**: For web viewing with interactivity

## Citation Management
Research Briefs should properly cite:
1. Brenner transcript sections (§42, §89, etc.)
2. External sources added to evidence ledger
3. Agent responses (which agent, which session)

## Templates
Users may want different Brief styles:
- **Executive Summary**: 1-page version for stakeholders
- **Technical Detail**: Full version with all data
- **Presentation Mode**: Slides-friendly structure
- **Academic Format**: Citation-ready for papers

## Dependencies
- All other components (aggregates their outputs)

## Success Criteria
- Research Briefs are genuinely useful for sharing
- Exports work across formats without data loss
- Citations are complete and verifiable
- Users actually export and share artifacts

 {"id":"brenner_bot-nu8g.1","title":"Design Research Brief Template","description":"# Design Research Brief Template

## Purpose
Create the template and structure for the primary output artifact: the Research Brief.

## Research Brief Sections

### 1. Metadata Header
```yaml
---
type: research_brief
version: 1.0
session_id: SM-2024-001
hypothesis_id: hyp-xyz
created_at: 2024-03-15T10:30:00Z
final_confidence: 37%
status: under_pressure
---
```

### 2. Executive Summary
2-3 sentences capturing:
- The hypothesis investigated
- Key finding (supported/challenged/unclear)
- Primary recommendation

### 3. Hypothesis Statement
Current formulation with:
- Statement
- Mechanism
- Domain

### 4. Hypothesis Evolution
If hypothesis changed during session:
- Original → Current transformation
- Trigger for each change
- Visual evolution diagram (optional)

### 5. Discriminative Structure
- Predictions if true
- Predictions if false
- Falsification conditions

### 6. Operators Applied
For each operator used:
- What was discovered
- Key outputs

### 7. Agent Analysis
- Devil's Advocate findings
- Experiment Designer protocols
- Brenner Channeler wisdom
- Consensus and conflicts

### 8. Evidence Summary
If evidence recorded:
- Tests run
- Results
- Confidence trajectory

### 9. Brenner Principles
Which transcript sections were relevant:
- §42: Level Split
- §89: Exclusion Test
- etc.

### 10. Recommended Next Steps
Prioritized actions:
1. High priority: [action]
2. Medium priority: [action]
3. etc.

## Template Engine
Use a template system that can:
- Fill from session data
- Handle missing sections gracefully
- Export to multiple formats

## File Location
`apps/web/src/lib/brenner-loop/artifacts/research-brief-template.ts`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All sections defined
- [ ] Template handles missing data
- [ ] Metadata schema complete
- [ ] Template readable and useful

 {"id":"brenner_bot-nu8g.2","title":"Implement Multi-Format Export (Markdown, PDF, JSON)","description":"# Implement Multi-Format Export (Markdown, PDF, JSON)

## Purpose
Allow users to export Research Briefs in multiple formats for different use cases.

## Export Formats

### Markdown (.md)
- Primary format
- Git-friendly
- Human-readable
- Can convert to other formats

```typescript
function exportToMarkdown(brief: ResearchBrief): string;
```

### PDF (.pdf)
- For sharing with non-technical audiences
- Print-friendly layout
- Professional appearance

Options:
- Browser print-to-PDF (simplest)
- Library like jsPDF or react-pdf
- Server-side generation with puppeteer (future)

```typescript
async function exportToPDF(brief: ResearchBrief): Promise<Blob>;
```

### JSON (.json)
- For programmatic use
- Import/export between sessions
- API responses

```typescript
function exportToJSON(brief: ResearchBrief): string;
```

### HTML (.html)
- Self-contained web view
- Email-friendly
- Interactive elements preserved (optional)

```typescript
function exportToHTML(brief: ResearchBrief): string;
```

## UI for Export
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Export Research Brief              │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                     │
│  Format:                            │
│  ○ Markdown (.md) - Recommended     │
│  ○ PDF (.pdf)                       │
│  ○ JSON (.json)                     │
│  ○ HTML (.html)                     │
│                                     │
│  Options:                           │
│  □ Include full agent responses     │
│  □ Include evidence details         │
│  □ Include evolution graph          │
│                                     │
│  [Cancel] [Export]                  │
│                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## File Location
`apps/web/src/lib/brenner-loop/artifacts/export.ts`
`apps/web/src/components/brenner-loop/artifacts/ExportModal.tsx`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Markdown export works perfectly
- [ ] PDF export produces readable document
- [ ] JSON export round-trips correctly
- [ ] UI provides clear options

-{"id":"brenner_bot-nu8g.3","title":"Build Citation Manager for Brenner References","description":"# Build Citation Manager for Brenner References

## Purpose
Properly cite Brenner transcript sections and external sources throughout artifacts.

## Citation Types

### 1. Brenner Transcript Citations
Format: §42, §89, §107, etc.

Each citation should link to:
- The transcript section
- A popup/tooltip with the quote
- Context in distillations if available

```typescript
interface BrennerCitation {
  section: number;
  quote: string;
  context?: string;
  operatorRelevance?: OperatorType[];
}
```

### 2. External Source Citations
User-added sources from Evidence Ledger:

```typescript
interface ExternalCitation {
  id: string;
  type: 'paper' | 'article' | 'book' | 'dataset' | 'observation' | 'other';
  title: string;
  authors?: string;
  year?: number;
  url?: string;
  doi?: string;
  notes?: string;
}
```

## Citation in Research Briefs
Each mention of Brenner insight should be cited:

```markdown
As Brenner noted, "What you need is an observation that would be
IMPOSSIBLE if you're wrong. Not merely unlikely — impossible" (§89).
```

## Citation Index
At the end of Research Brief:

```markdown
## References

### Brenner Transcript
- §42: Level separation principle
- §89: Falsification requirement
- §73: Chastity control concept

### External Sources
- Smith et al. (2023). "Social Media and Teen Mental Health." Journal...
- Instagram Algorithm Change Dataset (2021). Retrieved from...
```

## File Location
`apps/web/src/lib/brenner-loop/artifacts/citations.ts`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Brenner citations link to transcript
- [ ] External citations properly formatted
- [ ] Citation index generated
- [ ] Multiple formats supported

 {"id":"brenner_bot-o546","title":"TASK: Surface Relevant Corpus Quotes in Operator Sessions","description":"
## Make the Corpus ALIVE During Sessions

The Brenner corpus contains wisdom for every operator. During Level Split, show Brenner quotes about levels of explanation. During Exclusion Test, show quotes about designing tests.

### Integration Points

**Level Split (Σ) Operator**
- Surface quotes about "levels of explanation"
- Brenner on reductionism vs. holism
- Examples of level splitting from his work

**Exclusion Test (⊘) Operator**
- Quotes about discriminative tests
- "The test must be able to give a clean answer"
- Examples of tests that excluded alternatives

**Object Transpose (⟳) Operator**
- Quotes about changing reference frames
- Looking at problems from different angles
- His examples of productive transpositions

**Scale Check (⊙) Operator**
- Quotes about scale-dependence
- When effects change with scale
- His examples of scale-checking insights

### Implementation
- Use semantic search (ukd1.1) to find relevant quotes
- Query: [current hypothesis text] + [operator name]
- Show 2-3 most relevant quotes in sidebar
- Quote attribution with link to full transcript

### Quote Display
- Collapsible "Brenner Says" panel
- Quote text with context
- "Read more" link to corpus location
- User can favorite/bookmark quotes

### Depends On
- ukd1.1 (Vector Embeddings for Brenner Corpus)

 {"id":"brenner_bot-o7a","title":"Testing Infrastructure Epic","description":"Comprehensive testing infrastructure for brenner_bot project.

## Scope
This epic covers all testing needs:
- Unit testing with Vitest (Bun-compatible, no mocks philosophy)
- E2E testing with Playwright (full browser automation)
- Detailed logging and test reporting
- CI/CD integration

## Philosophy
- NO mocks or fakes - test real behavior
- Use real implementations, real data fixtures
- Detailed logging for debugging test failures
- Tests should be documentation

## Components to Test
- Web app: lib/ (parsers, auth, corpus-search, agent-mail-client)
- Web app: components (UI primitives, page components)
- Web app: pages and routes
- CLI: brenner.ts (argument parsing, mail commands)
- E2E: Full user flows through the application

## Success Criteria
- All critical paths have unit coverage
- E2E tests cover happy paths and error cases
- Tests run in CI on every PR
- Test failures have clear, actionable output","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-29T22:09:00.851985604-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-29T23:43:47.468037506-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-29T23:43:47.468037506-05:00","close_reason":"Testing Infrastructure Epic complete. All children closed: Unit tests for libraries, component tests, CLI tests, E2E tests, and CI/CD integration."}

 {"id":"brenner_bot-xlk2.4","title":"Implement Response Synthesis & Conflict Detection","description":"# Implement Response Synthesis & Conflict Detection

## Purpose
Analyze agent responses to identify points of agreement, disagreement, and generate a unified synthesis.

## Why Synthesis Matters
Three separate agent responses are valuable, but users also need:
- What do agents AGREE on?
- Where do they DISAGREE?
- What are the key takeaways?

## Synthesis Structure
```typescript
interface SynthesisResult {
  // Agreement
  consensusPoints: ConsensusPoint[];
  
  // Conflict
  conflictPoints: ConflictPoint[];
  
  // Recommendations
  recommendations: Recommendation[];
  
  // Brenner Principles Applied
  brennerPrinciples: AppliedPrinciple[];
  
  // Metadata
  synthesizedAt: Date;
  agentResponses: AgentResponse[];
}

interface ConsensusPoint {
  claim: string;
  supportingAgents: AgentRole[];
  strength: 'strong' | 'moderate' | 'weak';
}

interface ConflictPoint {
  topic: string;
  positions: { agent: AgentRole; position: string }[];
  resolution?: string;
}

interface Recommendation {
  action: string;
  priority: 'high' | 'medium' | 'low';
  rationale: string;
  suggestedBy: AgentRole[];
}
```

## Synthesis Logic
Can be done with:
1. **Heuristic Approach**: Pattern matching, keyword extraction
2. **Agent-Based**: Have a "Synthesis Agent" analyze other responses
3. **Hybrid**: Heuristics + optional LLM refinement

Start with heuristics, add agent-based later.

### Heuristic Approach
- Extract key claims from each response
- Compare claims for overlap (agreement)
- Identify contradictions (conflict)
- Surface actionable recommendations

## File Location
`apps/web/src/lib/brenner-loop/agents/synthesis.ts`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Consensus points extracted
- [ ] Conflicts identified
- [ ] Recommendations surfaced
- [ ] Synthesis is useful and readable

 {"id":"brenner_bot-xlk2.5","title":"Create Brenner Channeler Agent Persona","description":"# Create Brenner Channeler Agent Persona

## Purpose
Build the special "Brenner Would Say" agent that responds in character based on the transcript.

## Why This Agent is Special
The Brenner Channeler isn't just another critique agent. It channels Sydney Brenner's actual voice, drawing on:
- His specific turns of phrase
- His intellectual style
- His favorite examples
- His emphasis on discrimination vs confirmation

## Character Elements

### Voice & Style
From transcript analysis:
- Blunt, direct communication
- Wry humor, occasional sarcasm
- Reference to personal experiences
- Analogy-heavy explanations
- Provocative questions
- Impatience with sloppy thinking

### Core Themes to Emphasize
- "Not merely unlikely — impossible"
- The chastity control concept
- Level separation
- Mechanism vs correlation
- The value of being proven wrong

### Example Response Style
```
User: "I found a correlation of r=0.3, p<0.05. Is my hypothesis confirmed?"

Brenner Channeler:
"Confirmed? You've done nothing of the sort. You've found that
your data is not wildly inconsistent with your hypothesis.
That's a much weaker statement.

r=0.3 means you're explaining 9% of the variance. What's
explaining the other 91%? That's where the interesting
science is.

And p<0.05 just means you probably didn't get this result
by chance. It says nothing about whether your mechanism is
correct. A dozen different mechanisms could produce r=0.3.

What you need is an observation that would be IMPOSSIBLE if
you're wrong. Not merely unlikely — impossible. When you
find that, come back and we'll talk about 'confirmation.'

Until then, you have a correlation in search of a mechanism,
which is the very first step, not the last."

— Based on §89, §42, §73
```

## Implementation
- System prompt trained on transcript examples
- Citations back to specific sections
- Consistent persona across responses

## File Location
`apps/web/src/lib/brenner-loop/agents/prompts/brenner-channeler.md`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Responses sound like Brenner
- [ ] Citations to transcript included
- [ ] Core themes consistently emphasized
- [ ] Tone is appropriately provocative

 {"id":"brenner_bot-xlk2.6","title":"FEATURE: Objection Register - Track & Address Agent Challenges","description":"# Objection Register - Track & Address Agent Challenges

## The Gap This Fills
The Devil's Advocate and other agents raise objections, but currently:
- Objections are buried in prose responses
- No tracking of which objections have been addressed
- No accountability for unaddressed challenges
- Easy to ignore uncomfortable critiques

## Feature Design

### Objection Data Model
```typescript
interface Objection {
  id: string;
  sessionId: string;
  hypothesisId: string;
  
  // Source
  source: AgentRole | 'user' | 'external';
  sourceMessageId?: string;  // Link to original agent response
  
  // Content
  type: ObjectionType;
  summary: string;          // One-line summary
  fullArgument: string;     // Complete objection
  implications: string[];   // What follows if objection is valid
  
  // Severity
  severity: 'fatal' | 'serious' | 'moderate' | 'minor';
  
  // Status
  status: ObjectionStatus;
  response?: ObjectionResponse;
  
  // Metadata
  raisedAt: Date;
  addressedAt?: Date;
}

type ObjectionType = 
  | 'alternative_explanation'  // Y might be caused by Z instead
  | 'reverse_causation'        // Y might cause X
  | 'confound_identified'      // Unmeasured variable problem
  | 'measurement_issue'        // How you're measuring is flawed
  | 'selection_bias'           // Who you're studying is biased
  | 'logic_error'              // Reasoning doesn't follow
  | 'missing_evidence'         // Claim not supported
  | 'effect_size_concern'      // Too small to matter
  | 'generalization_problem'   // Doesn't apply broadly
  | 'other';

type ObjectionStatus = 
  | 'open'           // Not yet addressed
  | 'acknowledged'   // Recognized but not resolved
  | 'addressed'      // Response provided
  | 'dismissed'      // Explained why not relevant
  | 'accepted'       // Objection valid, hypothesis updated
  | 'testing'        // Being investigated

interface ObjectionResponse {
  responseType: 'refuted' | 'accepted' | 'partially_accepted' | 'testing' | 'dismissed';
  argument: string;
  evidence?: string;
  hypothesisUpdated: boolean;
  confidenceImpact?: number;  // How much did this change confidence?
}
```

### Key Features

#### 1. Automatic Extraction
Parse agent responses to extract objections:
- Identify challenge statements
- Classify by type
- Rate severity
- Create register entries

#### 2. Objection Dashboard
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  OBJECTION REGISTER                     3 open, 2 addressed │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  ⚠️ OPEN (requires response)                                │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │ 🔴 FATAL: Reverse Causation                          │ │
│  │ "Depression may cause increased social media use,    │ │
│  │  not the reverse" — Devil's Advocate                 │ │
│  │ [Address] [Accept] [Dismiss with reason]             │ │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │ 🟡 SERIOUS: Selection Bias                           │ │
│  │ "Users who report depression may have different      │ │
│  │  usage patterns pre-existing" — Brenner Channeler    │ │
│  │ [Address] [Accept] [Dismiss with reason]             │ │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                             │
│  ✅ ADDRESSED                                               │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │ ✓ Measurement Issue: "Self-report is unreliable"     │ │
│  │ Response: Refuted — Using validated PHQ-9 instrument │ │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### 3. Objection-Driven Testing
Each objection can spawn a test:
- "To address the reverse causation objection, I need a test that..."
- Links objection to test queue
- Resolves objection when test completes

#### 4. Research Brief Integration
Research Brief must include:
- All objections raised
- How each was addressed (or not)
- Outstanding objections clearly marked
- Intellectual honesty score

#### 5. Objection Patterns
Track across sessions:
- "This hypothesis type frequently faces reverse causation objections"
- "Users who dismiss objections have lower robustness scores"

### Why This is Critical
Without objection tracking:
- Users can ignore valid challenges
- Confirmation bias goes unchecked
- Research Brief lacks accountability
- The tribunal's value is diminished

With it:
- Every challenge is on the record
- Responses are documented
- Unaddressed objections are visible
- Intellectual honesty is enforced

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Objections extracted from agent responses
- [ ] Each objection requires explicit handling
- [ ] Responses are recorded and visible
- [ ] Research Brief shows objection status
- [ ] Outstanding objections prevent "complete" status

-{"id":"brenner_bot-xlk2.7","title":"FEATURE: Agent Debate Mode - Multi-Round Adversarial Dialogue","description":"# Agent Debate Mode - Multi-Round Adversarial Dialogue

## The Gap This Fills
Current design: Dispatch all agents → Wait → Collect responses → Synthesize

But this misses the value of DEBATE:
- Devil's Advocate challenges Experiment Designer's protocol
- Brenner Channeler responds to Devil's Advocate's objection
- Back-and-forth sharpens the analysis

## Feature Design

### Debate Structure
```typescript
interface AgentDebate {
  id: string;
  sessionId: string;
  topic: string;  // What's being debated
  
  // Participants
  participants: AgentRole[];
  moderator: 'system' | AgentRole;
  
  // Rounds
  rounds: DebateRound[];
  maxRounds: number;
  currentRound: number;
  
  // Outcome
  status: 'in_progress' | 'concluded' | 'timed_out';
  conclusion?: DebateConclusion;
}

interface DebateRound {
  number: number;
  speaker: AgentRole;
  content: string;
  replyingTo?: string;  // Which previous statement
  
  // Analysis
  newPointsMade: string[];
  objectionsRaised: string[];
  concessionsGiven: string[];
}

interface DebateConclusion {
  consensus: string[];       // What all agents agreed on
  unresolved: string[];      // Genuine disagreements
  keyInsight: string;        // Most important takeaway
  winningArguments: string[];// Strongest points made
}
```

### Key Features

#### 1. Structured Debate Formats

**Format A: Oxford-Style**
- Motion: "This hypothesis is falsifiable"
- Proposition: Experiment Designer argues for
- Opposition: Devil's Advocate argues against
- Judge: Brenner Channeler

**Format B: Socratic Questioning**
- Brenner Channeler asks probing questions
- Other agents must respond
- Reveals weak points through dialogue

**Format C: Steel-Manning Contest**
- Each agent must steelman the hypothesis
- Then each agent tries to defeat their own steelman
- Best attack wins

#### 2. Debate Viewer
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  AGENT DEBATE: Is this hypothesis falsifiable?              │
│  Round 3 of 5                                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                             │
│  🟢 Experiment Designer (Round 1):                          │
│  "I propose a three-arm RCT that would definitively test..." │
│                                                             │
│  🔴 Devil's Advocate (Round 2):                             │
│  "Your RCT has a fatal flaw: you can't blind participants   │
│   to which arm they're in. They'll know if they're getting  │
│   the algorithmic feed or not. Demand characteristics       │
│   invalidate the design."                                   │
│                                                             │
│  🟢 Experiment Designer (Round 3):                          │
│  "Fair point. I modify my proposal: instead of removing     │
│   the algorithm, we modify it subtly. Participants won't    │
│   know which variant they're receiving..."                  │
│                                                             │
│  ⏳ Waiting for 🔴 Devil's Advocate...                       │
│                                                             │
│  [Pause Debate] [Skip to Conclusion] [Inject Question]      │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### 3. User Injection
User can inject questions or challenges mid-debate:
- "Devil's Advocate, what about selection bias?"
- Forces the debate to address specific concerns

#### 4. Debate Outcomes
After debate concludes:
- Extract all objections to Objection Register
- Extract all experimental designs
- Identify where debate resolved disagreement
- Flag persistent disagreements

### Integration Points
- Agent Tribunal → Enhanced with debate option
- Objection Register → Populated from debate
- Test Queue → Experimental designs added

## Why This is Critical
Single-round responses are shallow. Debate:
- Sharpens arguments through opposition
- Reveals weaknesses in real-time
- Produces refined experimental designs
- More closely mirrors how good research happens

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Multiple debate formats available
- [ ] Multi-round exchanges work
- [ ] User can inject questions
- [ ] Conclusions properly extracted
- [ ] Debate transcript preserved

 {"id":"brenner_bot-xq4","title":"Web: rawContent fallback for unparsable corpus docs","description":"When a corpus markdown file lacks the expected section structure (e.g., no PART headers / no '## N. Title' headers), our viewers currently render blank.

Add a raw markdown fallback so pages remain readable:
- parsers expose rawContent when structured parse yields 0 sections/parts
- viewers render rawContent as simple paragraphs (no fancy structure)

Acceptance:
- distillation + transcript docs never render an empty page
- existing structured docs render unchanged
